description: Three-speaker panel: two same-gender females (amy + kristin) plus a male (ryan) moderator. Natural flow with interjections and short overlaps — the kind of dynamics a real podcast or panel show has. Tests whether the diarizer can hold three stable clusters even when same-gender voices alternate quickly.

turns.speaker: mod
turns.text: Welcome to the panel. Today we're talking about real time speech diarization.

turns.speaker: mod
turns.text: Let's start with what makes this problem hard. Why is it so much harder than transcription alone?

turns.speaker: panel_a
turns.text: Transcription only has to find the words. Diarization has to figure out who said which words, in real time, which is a much harder constraint.

turns.speaker: panel_b
turns.text: Right, and overlap makes it even worse.

turns.speaker: mod
turns.text: Where do you see the failure modes most often?

turns.speaker: panel_a
turns.text: When two speakers have similar voices. Same gender, similar age, similar accent.

turns.speaker: panel_b
turns.text: And on top of that, when one speaker only contributes a quick word here and there.

turns.speaker: mod
turns.text: Like a moderator's hmm or right.

turns.speaker: panel_a
turns.text: Yeah, those brief tokens are notoriously hard to attribute.

turns.speaker: panel_b
turns.text: So what do you think the path forward is?

turns.speaker: panel_a
turns.text: Better embedding models trained specifically on conversational data, not just enrollment style speech.
